title: Repeaters & Signal Regeneration
description: Layer 1 devices that regenerate degraded signals to extend network reach beyond cable distance limits.
fullExplanation: As signals travel through cables, they attenuate (weaken) and accumulate noise. A repeater receives the degraded signal, cleans it up, and retransmits it at full strength. Repeaters operate purely at Layer 1 — they have no concept of MAC addresses, IP addresses, or protocols. They simply amplify or regenerate the physical signal. Modern networks rarely use standalone repeaters — switches (Layer 2) effectively repeat signals on each port.

types.name: Analog Repeater (Amplifier)
types.description: Amplifies the signal including any noise. Used in older coax networks and cable TV.

types.name: Digital Repeater (Regenerator)
types.description: Samples the signal, decides 0 or 1, retransmits clean. Eliminates accumulated noise. Used in fiber and modern copper.

types.name: Optical Amplifier (EDFA)
types.description: Amplifies light signal directly without converting to electrical. Used in long-haul fiber (every 80-100km).

types.name: WiFi Range Extender
types.description: Receives WiFi signal and retransmits. Effectively a wireless repeater. Halves throughput (receive + retransmit on same channel).

keyFacts: Repeaters extend cable segments beyond 100m limit
Operate at Layer 1 only — no MAC/IP awareness
Digital repeaters eliminate noise accumulation (unlike analog amplifiers)
5-4-3 rule (10BASE-T): max 5 segments, 4 repeaters, 3 populated segments
Switches replaced hubs/repeaters in modern networks
EDFA (Erbium-Doped Fiber Amplifier): optical amplifier for long-haul fiber
WiFi mesh systems use wireless backhaul instead of repeaters to avoid throughput loss
